claim: Depending on the dog, there is a 50–99% probability that a lost dog is not exhibiting normal expected behaviors, making pre-search temperament assessment critical.
stance_target: behavioral regression prevalence among lost dogs
scope_conditions: lost dogs generally; percentage range reflects individual variation
quoted_support: Depending on your dog's history, there is a 50-75-90-99% chance that your dog, being lost, is not exhibiting the same behaviors you would normally expect.

claim: In survival mode, all humans — including the dog's long-term owner who has fed, loved, and cared for it for years — are perceived as predators.
stance_target: owner recognition failure mechanism in fear state
scope_conditions: dogs that have fully entered survival mode
quoted_support: When a dog has entered survival mode, very often EVERY human is viewed as a predator – even the one that has fed, loved, walked, bathed and pampered them for the last 2-4-12 years.

claim: In 95%+ of survival-mode dogs, encounters with stimuli (voices, shaking food containers, squeaky toys, other dogs barking, cars, slamming doors) produce a flight response rather than fight response.
stance_target: predominance of flight over fight in lost-dog fear state
scope_conditions: dogs in survival mode exposed to stimuli
quoted_support: People yelling (calling a pets name), shaking a food container (loud noises), cars, other dogs barking and pursuing, squeeking toys, a door slamming – these will all invite a fight or flight response. In most (95%+) this will result in a flight response rather than fight.

claim: When a dog in survival mode encounters a familiar sight, smell, or sound, it may paradoxically leave the area and avoid returning, creating a negative connotation to that location.
stance_target: counterproductive effect of owner presence at last-seen location
scope_conditions: dogs in full survival mode with strong owner bond creating cognitive conflict
quoted_support: When a dog in survival mode sees, hears or smells something familiar to them, they will do the exact opposite of what you would expect. They will leave the area and avoid it in the future, as there will now be a negative connotation to that particular spot.

claim: There is very little correlation between a dog's prior behavior or life history and whether or how quickly it will regress into survival mode when lost.
stance_target: unpredictability of survival mode onset regardless of prior temperament
quoted_support: There is very little correlation with previous behavior/life history to regression into this mode, though certain breeds and personality profiles are more prone to an immediate transition when they get lost.

claim: A well-adjusted, well-trained, well-loved dog can enter survival mode ('turn skittish') in moments — it is not the owner's fault, nor the dog's fault.
stance_target: framing of survival mode as instinctual not behavioral failure
scope_conditions: any lost dog, any prior training level
quoted_support: A well adjusted, well trained, well loved dog can turn 'skittish' in a heartbeat. It IS NOT the owners fault. It IS NOT the dogs fault.
